PDFQA: Retrieval-augmented generative question
answering system for educational PDFs
Thumma Sritejaswini Reddy
Department of Computer
Science and Engineering
Anurag University
Hyderabad,Telangana
22eg105d55@anurag.edu.in
Kore Dinesh Kumar
Department of Computer

Anurag University
Hyderabad,Telangana

Anurag University

23eg505d03@anurag.edu.in

Assistant Professor
Department of Computer
Science and Engineering
Anurag University
Hyderabad, Telangana
Purnacharycse@anurag.edu.in
Abstract—Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solutions
are a newly emerging best practice for storing Large Language
Model (LLM) answers in a manner grounded in factual in-
formation contained in user-supplied content (e.g., documents

1.content:
• For local vector storage, OmniRAG uses FAISS’s index-
ing and vectoring capabilities with flat indexes and per-
forms cosine similarity searches, allowing text to match
against images and vice versa. It enables cross-modal
retrieval or effective searches of the document space
irrespective of modality, with many possible matching
instances (text-image or image-text).
• An agentic reasoning loop is based on the Lang-
Graph framework, within which the LLM can choose
to
invoke
either
the
retrieve_documents
or
search_chat_history tools based upon query clas-
sification.
• A complete production stack with a full Next.js frontend
implementing a chatbot style interface for user interaction
and a FastAPI backend interacts with an LLM based upon
the Qwen3-VL vision-language model executed on local
infrastructure via Ollama.
II. RELATED WORK
A. Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Lewis et al. [3] created a Retrieval-Augmented Gener-
ation (RAG) method that entails combining a pre-trained
sequence-to

3.content:
A. Frontend Layer
The Next.js 14 frontend uses the App Router, React,
TypeScript, and TailwindCSS to create a unique style single-
page application with a sidebar that allows users to manage
chat history (create, select, delete), and a central chat panel
that supports the inline upload of documents. Assistant user
responses are displayed using react-markdown with remark-
gfm for proper formatting of tables, headers, and code blocks
(1). Communication between the frontend and backend occurs
via an extended REST API request-response mechanism (600
seconds) to support the long inference times of larger models
used for inferencing.
B. Backend Layer
The backend is built with Python version 3.10 using FastAPI
and Uvicorn.
There are three major groups of endpoints:
• /upload: This endpoint will take in both PDFs and
image files, send them to the proper processor, and store
the embeddings in FAISS.
• /chats: This endpoint provides the functionality to
perform CRUD operations related to your ch

5.content:
system prompt, conversation history, and current ques-
tion and any associated media.
2) Tool Node: Executes calls to tools requested by the
Reasoner. There are two available tools:
• retrieve_documents(query): Encodes the
query using the CLIP text encoder and searches for
similar docu- ments in a FAISS-generic way and
returns the three most similar documents and their
metadata.
• search_chat_history(query):
Searches
through the entire history of chat sessions and
retrieves previous messages that match the query
using keyword matching.
The agent model has a conditional edge structure; after a
reasoner produces a response, the tools condition function is
called to check if tool calls were made and, if so, the Tool
Node is called to execute the tools and return their results
to the Reasoner for a second pass. If no tool executions are
required, the response is returned directly to the user.
The system prompt implements a query classification strat-
egy that directs the LLM to:
• Directly

7.content:
The baseline of Text-Only BM25 fails entirely on any query
related to an image due to their inability to process visual
content when retrieving. In comparison, Dense text retrieval
can retrieve text in someway better than BM25 because it
has the ability to also take into consideration the semantic
similarity of the documents but is that there is still no way for
them to retrieve images as well. The separate encoder approach
uses two different encoding tools to classify and encode
images and text respectively. This leads to each space (text
and image) being misaligned, resulting in a lower accuracy of
cross-modal retrievals. OmniRAG does not suffer from this
issue because it uses a single CLIP embedding space for
encoding both text and images respectively, allowing for the
direct comparison of text and image vectors which produces
the highest retrieval accuracy of 89.0%.
C. Agent Tool-Calling Behavior
The agentic architecture was tested against the capability of
the intelligent system (

19.content:
Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented
Generation System
Scott Barnett, Stefanus Kurniawan, Srikanth Thudumu, Zach Brannelly, Mohamed Abdelrazek
{scott.barnett,stefanus.kurniawan,srikanth.thudumu,zach.brannelly,mohamed.abdelrazek}@deakin.edu.au
Applied Artificial Intelligence Institute
Geelong, Australia
ABSTRACT
Software engineers are increasingly adding semantic search capabil-
ities to applications using a strategy known as Retrieval Augmented
Generation (RAG). A RAG system involves finding documents that
semantically match a query and then passing the documents to a
large language model (LLM) such as ChatGPT to extract the right
answer using an LLM. RAG systems aim to: a) reduce the problem
of hallucinated responses from LLMs, b) link sources/references
to generated responses, and c) remove the need for annotating
documents with meta-data. However, RAG systems suffer from lim-
itations inherent to information retrieval systems and from reliance
on LLMs. In this

20.content:
CAIN 2024, April 2024, Lisbon, Portugal
Scott Barnett, Stefanus Kurniawan, Srikanth Thudumu, Zach Brannelly, Mohamed Abdelrazek
and answer pairs. We indexed all documents then ran the
queries and stored the generated responses using GPT-4. All
question and answer pairs were then validated with OpenAI
evals 1. Manual inspection (all discrepancies, all flagged as
incorrect, and a sample of correct labels) was analysed to
identify the patterns.
• What are the key considerations when engineering a RAG
system? (section 6) We present the lessons learned from three
case studies involving the implementation of a RAG system.
This presents the challenges faced and insights gained.
Contributions arising from this work include:
• A catalogue of failure points (FP) that occur in RAG systems.
• An experience report from 3 case studies of implementing a
RAG system. Two currently running at Deakin University.
• A research direction for RAG systems based on the lessons
learned from the 3 case studies.

21.content:
Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System
CAIN 2024, April 2024, Lisbon, Portugal
Figure 1: Indexing and Query processes required for creating a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) system. The indexing
process is typically done at development time and queries at runtime. Failure points identified in this study are shown in red
boxes. All required stages are underlined. Figure expanded from [19].
The final stage of a RAG pipeline is when the answer is extracted
from the generated text. Readers are responsible for filtering the
noise from the prompt, adhering to formatting instructions (i.e. an-
swer the question as a list of options), and producing the output to
return for the query. Implementation of a RAG system requires cus-
tomising multiple prompts to process questions and answers. This
process ensures that questions relevant for the domain are returned.
The use of large language models to answer real time questions
from documents opens up new a

24.content:
Seven Failure Points When Engineering a Retrieval Augmented Generation System
CAIN 2024, April 2024, Lisbon, Portugal
FP
Lesson
Description
Case Studies
FP4
Larger context get better results (Context refers to a
particular setting or situation in which the content
occurs)
A larger context enabled more accurate responses
(8K vs 4K). Contrary to prior work with GPT-3.5 [13]
AI Tutor
FP1
Semantic caching drives cost and latency down
RAG systems struggle with concurrent users due to
rate limits and the cost of LLMs. Prepopulate the
semantic cache with frequently asked questions [1].
AI Tutor
FP5-7
Jailbreaks bypass the RAG system and hit the safety
training.
Research suggests fine-tuning LLMs reverses safety
training [11], test all fine-tuned LLMs for RAG sys-
tem.
AI Tutor
FP2, FP4
Adding meta-data improves retrieval.
Adding the file name and chunk number into the
retrieved context helped the reader extract the re-
quired information. Useful for chat dialogue.
AI Tutor
FP2, FP4-7
Open sou
